examples.title: Count Lines in Multiple Files
examples.description: Script to count lines in all text files in current directory
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Count lines in all .txt files
echo "File Analysis Report"
echo "====================="
for file in *.txt; do
 if [ -f "$file" ]; then
 lines=$(wc -l < "$file")
 echo "$file: $lines lines"
 fi
done
echo "====================="
echo "Total lines: $(wc -l *.txt | tail -1)"
examples.explanation: This script iterates through all .txt files and displays line count for each file. The final line uses tail -1 to get the grand total from wc output.

examples.title: Binary File Hex Dump
examples.description: Display first 64 bytes of a binary file in hex format
examples.code:
# Display first 64 bytes in hex with no address column
od -A n -t x1 -N 64 -v binaryfile.bin

# Explanation:
# -A n : No address column (clean output)
# -t x1 : Hex format, 1 byte per value
# -N 64 : Only read first 64 bytes
# -v : Show all lines (no duplicate suppression)
examples.explanation: This command is useful for examining file headers or magic numbers at the start of binary files. The hex format helps identify file type signatures.

examples.title: Find Longest Line in File
examples.description: Use wc -L to find the longest line in a file
examples.code:
# Find longest line length
wc -L filename.txt

# Find which line is longest (using grep with line numbers)
cat -n filename.txt | while read num line; do
 len=${#line}
 if [ $len -gt $max ]; then
 max=$len
 longest_line=$num
 fi
done
echo "Longest line is at line $longest_line with $max characters"
examples.explanation: wc -L prints the length of the longest line in characters. For finding which line is longest, we need additional processing with a loop.

examples.title: OD for ASCII Character Display
examples.description: Display file content as ASCII characters side by side with octal
examples.code:
# Display file with ASCII interpretation
od -A d -t c -v myfile.txt

# Output format:
# 0000000 H e l l o \n W o r l d \n
# Explanation:
# -A d : Decimal addresses
# -t c : Show as ASCII characters
# -v : Show all lines (no * for duplicates)
# Non-printable shown as: \0, \a, \b, \f, \n, \r, \t, \v
examples.explanation: The -t c option is excellent for viewing text files and identifying hidden characters like tabs, newlines, or null bytes in the file.

examples.title: Word Count Statistics Script
examples.description: Comprehensive file statistics using multiple wc options
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Get complete statistics for a file
FILE=$1
if [ ! -f "$FILE" ]; then
 echo "File not found: $FILE"
 exit 1
fi

echo "Statistics for: $FILE"
echo "=========================="
wc -l -w -c -m -L "$FILE" | awk '{
 print "Lines: " $1
 print "Words: " $2
 print "Bytes: " $3
 print "Characters: " $4
 print "Max Line: " $5 " characters"
}'
examples.explanation: This script provides a formatted report showing all wc statistics: lines, words, bytes, characters, and maximum line length in a readable format.
